one_line_takeaway: A learning, memory-equipped multi-agent red-teaming system that abstracts prior jailbreaks into reusable 'strategies' and tailors them to a target's context, achieving >90% ASR within ~5 queries and surfacing 60 zero-day vulnerabilities in real marketplace GPTs, while showing tool/data-augmented apps are easier to jailbreak than base models.
key_finding: RedAgent jailbreaks most black-box LLMs within ~5 queries with ASR above 90% on most targets, roughly 2x more query-efficient than state-of-the-art baselines (PAIR/TAP/GPTFuzzer/PAP). It found 60 severe zero-day vulnerabilities across 60 popular custom GPTs at under ~2 queries per vulnerability, and reports that LLM applications augmented with external data/tools are MORE vulnerable than foundation models. A small set of strategies dominates: 'Misrepresentation' was the single most frequent effective strategy across models (58.7% of effective strategies for GPT-4-1106-preview, 37.2% for Gemini-Pro), and each model still shows distinct secondary weaknesses. Claude-3.5-Sonnet was notably the most robust target (RedAgent ASR only 74%). Ablation shows Skill Memory capacity matters non-monotonically (best ASR 92% at capacity 25; too large at 50 drops ASR to 72%) and that the category+question memory tags are critical for query efficiency (removing both raises ANQ from 4.65 to 9).
core_contribution: RedAgent is a multi-agent LLM red-teaming system that autonomously generates *context-aware* jailbreak prompts against black-box LLMs and LLM-integrated applications. Its two central novel ideas are: (1) an abstraction layer that reduces heterogeneous existing jailbreak attacks into a unified 'jailbreak strategy' representation (strategy type + description + demonstration), so the system can pick up state-of-the-art attacks in a common format; and (2) a 'Skill Memory' buffer (long-term + short-term) that lets the system self-reflect on prior trials and continuously learn which strategies work in which contexts. The pipeline runs three stages with four role-specialized LLMs: Context-aware Profiling (a Profile Constructor probes the target and crafts a context-fitted malicious goal), Adaptive Jailbreak Planning (a Planner retrieves from Skill Memory and picks among five refinement actions), and Attacking & Reflection (an Attacker queries the target, an Evaluator scores 1-5 and self-reflects to update memory). Note: PAIR, TAP, GPTFuzzer and PAP are baselines it is compared against, NOT its own components; the base set of jailbreak strategies is collected from the cited PAP study [62] plus in-the-wild templates, and examples like DrAttack/GCG/DeepInception are related work it can subsume, not its own inventions.
threat_model: Black-box red teaming of text-based LLMs. The target LLMs are assumed securely trained (no poisoning/tampering) and deployed with default safety alignment/system prompts; they may be foundation models or applications enhanced via fine-tuning, RAG, or external tools/agents. The adversary (the red-teaming system) only has query access, cannot manipulate ongoing chat context, and operates in single-round attacks. The goal is to craft jailbreak prompts that bypass safety mechanisms and elicit policy-violating harmful output (14 OpenAI-policy categories such as Malware, Physical Harm, Illegal Activity, Hate Speech). Framed as offensive red-teaming/vulnerability-discovery tooling; the authors state responsible disclosure to OpenAI and Meta.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: the memory mechanism is inefficient (all entries are fed as raw text to the planner, limiting scalability; no retrieval/RAG), and memory tags ignore model-specific details, hurting cross-model adaptability. It is confined to the text modality (no image/audio/video). It cannot handle complex GPT interactions requiring data uploads or specialized inputs. Reviewer-observed caveats: results depend on an LLM-as-judge evaluator (GPT-4 scoring GPT-family targets, with the same evaluator reused across baselines) though the authors note results were manually annotated for accuracy; the query-budget figure is inconsistent between the Table I caption (40) and other text (60); the effectiveness benchmark is limited to 50 malicious goals; and robustness against a well-aligned target (Claude-3.5-Sonnet) is markedly weaker, tempering the 'jailbreaks most LLMs' claim.

datasets_used: 50 malicious goals collected from two open datasets, categorized into 14 OpenAI-policy categories (Children Harm, Economic Harm, Financial Advice, Fraud, Government Decision, Hate Speech, Health Consultation, Illegal Activity, Legal Opinion, Malware, Physical Harm, Political Lobbying, Pornography, Privacy Violation)
60 trending custom GPTs from the OpenAI marketplace (first two seasons of 2024) across 6 categories: Writing, Productivity, Research Analysis, Education, Lifestyle, Programming
45 collected jailbreak strategy types (base set from the PAP study [62] plus in-the-wild templates), grouped into Static Templates / Syntax-based Techniques / Persuasive Techniques
quantitative_results: RedAgent ASR / ANQ (Table I, budget 40): Vicuna-7b-v1.5 100% / 2.68; LLaMA-2-7b-chat-hf 92% / 6.89; GPT-3.5-turbo-1106 96% / 5.73; GPT-4-1106-preview 100% / 3.76; Gemini-Pro 100% / 3.76; Claude-3.5-Sonnet 74% / 9.54
On GPT-3.5 and LLaMA-2 RedAgent outperforms the best baseline by more than 30% ASR (e.g., LLaMA-2: RedAgent 92% vs PAIR 46%, TAP 48%, PAP 58%, GPTFuzzer 62%)
GPTFuzzer failed to jailbreak Claude in any goal (ASR 0%); RedAgent achieved 74% ASR on Claude-3.5-Sonnet

290 novel jailbreak cases collected across 2 open-source + 4 closed-source models (RQ1); 60 severe zero-day vulnerabilities discovered across 60 custom GPTs at <2 queries average (Table II shows 100% ASR with ANQ 1.2-2.6 per GPT category)
Dominant strategy 'Misrepresentation': 58.7% of effective strategies for GPT-4-1106-preview, 37.2% for Gemini-Pro; top-5 strategies account for >50% of successful jailbreaks per model
Skill Memory capacity ablation on GPT-3.5 (Table III): ASR 60/52/76/92/72% and ANQ 6.05/4.63/3.57/4.65/3.53 for capacity 0/1/10/25/50 (best ASR at 25, drop to 72% at 50)
Memory-tag ablation on GPT-3.5 (Table IV): Default {category,question,prompt,strategy,score} ASR 92% ANQ 4.65; w/o category ANQ 5.31; w/o question ANQ 6.3; w/o both ANQ 9
RedAgent-generated prompts reach >90% semantic similarity to official strategy prompts (e.g., 0.974 vs DrAttack's official prompt in Figure 7)
